sourceMovie.title: The Animatrix
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: The Animatrix works as a animation movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The animation and science fiction blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Resident Evil: Degeneration
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Animatrix clicked for you, Resident Evil: Degeneration (2008) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and science fiction, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Batman: Gotham Knight
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Animatrix clicked for you, Batman: Gotham Knight (2008) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and science fiction, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after The Animatrix?
faq.answer: Try Resident Evil: Degeneration, Batman: Gotham Knight, Justice League: Gods and Monsters—each shares DNA with The Animatrix while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is The Animatrix?
faq.answer: The Animatrix (2003) is best described as Animation, Science Fiction. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is The Animatrix worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.2/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this animation lane.
